communityVoices.quote: I'm gonna go out on a limb and say the Jam at the end of Weather Report Suite is "THE BEST JAM EVER" by any band.

it evolves into the most beautifully rendered and involving MLB Jam ever and then goes through multiple different tempo's, styles and landscapes with the entire band contributing, all telepathically on the same magical wavelength throughout

the only other mind-left-body jam i can think of that comes remotely within the same galaxy as this one is 7/31/74, but its way shorter and doesn't go through the mindbogglingly telepathic changes as this does
This was my first Dick's Picks (a gift from a friend) - I was not dissapointed (what an understatement!). You won't be either.
Dick had to fight tooth and nail to get any show released.

whyThisShow: The broad community case for this show rests almost entirely on one sequence: the Weather Report Suite dissolving into the Mind Left Body Jam and beyond. One reviewer called it "THE BEST JAM EVER" by any band [source: 'I'm gonna go out on a limb and say the Jam at the end of Weather Report Suite is "THE BEST JAM EVER" by any band']. Another broke the sequence into seven distinct movements spanning roughly 28 minutes, including a Dark Star jam fragment, a two-chord minor excursion, and multiple jazzy peaks before the whole thing collapses into U.S. Blues [source: 'jam (spacey noodling, mostly): ~4:45 (4:45) MLB jam: ~5:30 (10:15) Dark Star jam: ~4:00 (11:15) jam (2 chords): ~5:00 (16:15) jam (jazzy, multiple themes): ~4:30 (20:45) space-jam: ~5:00 (25:45) jam (jazzy): ~3:15 (28:00)']. The rest of the setlist gets less ink from reviewers, though one notes a "really hot Scarlet" and what they call "the best U.S. blues ever" bookending the night [source: 'a really hot Scarlet, GDTRFB, and also the best U.S. blues ever, which emerges from the aforementioned JAM']. This is a soundboard recording released officially as Dick's Picks Vol. 12, and the community signal is strong: nearly every reviewer treats the WRS jam as a once-in-a-lifetime document.
listenFor: The WRS>jam sequence is the whole reason to queue this up. Coming out of a "terrific WRS," the jam moves through "dark ashes of space" before locking into the MLB theme [source: 'From the dark ashes of space following a terrific WRS it evolves into the most beautifully rendered and involving MLB Jam ever']. What distinguishes this version from other 1974 MLB jams is that it "goes through multiple different tempo's, styles and landscapes with the entire band contributing" without dropping the thread [source: 'goes through multiple different tempo's, styles and landscapes with the entire band contributing, all telepathically on the same magical wavelength throughout, never losing any momentum']. One reviewer who listened closely heard a Dark Star theme surface briefly inside the jam — "oh, if only they'd played it all!" — before it gives way to further territory [source: 'it's into a sweet, sweet Dark Star jam (oh, if only they'd played it all!)']. The Phil and Ned Seastones interlude in set one is noted on the setlist but draws no specific community commentary in the sampled reviews; the WRS sequence is where listener attention concentrates.
context: June 1974 puts the band deep in the Wall of Sound era, hauling the massive PA system into arenas across the country. Jerry reportedly held this period in high regard: one reviewer noted that "Jerry often mentioned that the Spring/Summer '74 tours were his favorites" [source: 'Jerry often mentioned that the Spring/Summer '74 tours were his favorites']. The show was released as Dick's Picks Vol. 12, a release that reportedly required some internal struggle to bring out, with one reviewer noting that "Dick had to fight tooth and nail to get any show released" [source: 'Dick had to fight tooth and nail to get any show released']. The Archive identifier reflects a soundboard source (gd74-06-28.set1.sbd.snider.10984.sbeok.shnf), meaning the recording quality matches the canonical status of the performance.
funFact: One reviewer mapped out the full post-WRS jam in seven timed segments, clocking the entire sequence at approximately 28 minutes — and still called it an understatement to say they weren't disappointed [source: 'I was not dissapointed (what an understatement!)'].
headline: Boston Garden, June '74, and that jam.
